summary:
The Fall of the Western Roman Empire (conventionally 476 AD) is one of the most studied transitions in world history — the process by which the western half of the greatest empire in the ancient world dissolved into a patchwork of Germanic kingdoms over the course of roughly a century, ending the political unity of the Mediterranean world that Rome had maintained for 500 years. The process was gradual, complex, and debated by historians for centuries: Edward Gibbon's 'Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire' (1776) attributed it to Christianity and barbarism; modern scholars emphasize fiscal exhaustion, the limits of Roman military capacity, climate change, disease, and the complexity of governing a vast pre-modern empire.

The key milestones in the collapse included: the [[event:2793|Battle of Adrianople]] (378), which established Germanic peoples as autonomous forces within imperial territory; the [[event:2033|Sack of Rome by Visigoths]] (410), which shattered the psychological myth of Roman invulnerability; the crossing of the Rhine by multiple Germanic peoples on New Year's Eve 406 AD; the [[event:2037|Vandal sack of Rome]] (455); and the [[event:2861|deposition of Romulus Augustulus]] by Odoacer (476). The Western Empire's administrative capacity had been undermined by a century of civil wars, military coups, tax revolts, and the progressive transfer of military power to Germanic foederati who had no particular loyalty to Rome as an abstract ideal.

The fall's consequences shaped everything that followed: the Catholic Church became the primary institutional continuity between Rome and medieval Europe; Latin evolved into the Romance languages; Roman law became the foundation of Western legal systems; and the memory of Rome haunted European political thought for a thousand years — the Holy Roman Empire, the Byzantine Empire's claims, and eventually Napoleon all defined themselves in relation to Rome's legacy.
